coredata.dc:title: Opening band gap of multi-color graphene quantum dots using D-fructose as a green precursor
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 THE AUTHORS. Published by Elsevier BV on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Alexandria University.

coredata.dc:creator: Al Ghamdi, Haifa S.
Al-Ghamdi, Ahmed A.
coredata.dc:description: Graphene was successfully isolated by Professors Andre Geim and Kostya Novoselov in 2004. Graphene has attracted considerable research importance for its distinctive properties and potential applications. In this study, low-cost and high blue, yellow, and green photoluminescent (PL) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized with deionized water (DI) and D-fructose as precursors by the dehydration method in the presence of hydrochloric acid. Transmission electron microscopy imaging proved the changing size of the GQDs and, by nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy, we observed the hydrogen-functionalized GQDs. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the synthesized GQDs were determined, and an energy bandgap was obtained from optical investigations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: multicolor GQDs
Band gap: cyclic voltammetry
D-fructose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design, Synthesis, and Mechanism of the GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the formation mechanism of GQDs via an HCl dehydration method at 85 °C for 2 h.
(a) XRD of precursor material powder D-fructose and (b) XRD for prepared GQDs at various sizes.
UV–Vis spectra of GQDs corresponding to the growth of GQDs.
(a) PL spectra of GQD samples and (b) photos of GQD samples in the dark box.
The spectra of GQDs PL emission during various excitation wavelengths.
FT-IR spectra of the GQD samples.
(a-c) TEM images of GQDs and corresponding size distribution histograms of GQDs; (d-f) high-resolution TEM images of each GQD size.

(a) Tauc's plot of GQDs at various sizes; (b) the size distribution of GQDs corresponding to
E g
.

1H NMR spectrum of GQDs in Deuterium (
D 2
O).

13C NMR spectrum of GQDs in Deuterium (
D 2
O).

(a) Cyclic voltammetry measurements of the multicolor GQDs/glassy carbon and bare glassy carbon electrodes; (b) positions of HOMO and LUMO energy states of the multicolor GQDs against vacuum and Ag/AgCl.
The size distribution of GQDs corresponds to Eg (A) and Eg (PL).

head.ce:title: Opening band gap of multi-color graphene quantum dots using D-fructose as a green precursor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, King Abdulaziz University, 21589, Jeddah, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, King Abdulaziz University, 21589, Jeddah, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Jeddah, College of Science, Department of Physics, Jeddah, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Jeddah, College of Science, Department of Physics, Jeddah, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene was successfully isolated by Professors Andre Geim and Kostya Novoselov in 2004. Graphene has attracted considerable research importance for its distinctive properties and potential applications. In this study, low-cost and high blue, yellow, and green photoluminescent (PL) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized with deionized water (DI) and D-fructose as precursors by the dehydration method in the presence of hydrochloric acid. Transmission electron microscopy imaging proved the changing size of the GQDs and, by nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy, we observed the hydrogen-functionalized GQDs. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the synthesized GQDs were determined, and an energy bandgap was obtained from optical investigations.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is considered the top and most promising material in many areas owing to its beneficial and attractive structure; its composition is a one-atom thick, hexagonal layer of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms [1]. Graphene alone has zero bandgaps and therefore cannot be used as a luminescent element for optoelectronic applications [2].

Graphene-to-graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are receiving immense attention from scientists in capitalizing on graphene properties. This feature is achieved when synthesizing zero-dimensional (0D) GQDs with a diameter of less than 10 nm indicating stronger quantum confinement and obvious edge effects [2,3]. Scientists are still having difficulty isolating (GQDs), because of the high cost and limited yield, which presents difficulties for practical applications [4]. All these reasons drive us to think of simple, unique methods, with straightforward and easy tools. Graphene and graphene derivatives can be prepared in several ways, some of which are technically complicated or time-consuming [5]. The dehydration method for organic materials is one of the best-proposed methods for the manufacturing of graphene [6,7]. When designing dehydration methods for organic materials, many factors must be considered, particularly in the manufacture of nanomaterials, and the final process design is influenced by factors such as chemical stability, stability of crystal shape, agglomerating, granulation, and unwanted particle attrition [8]. These many factors mean that many potentially useful drying materials vary in effectiveness in the process of dehydrating organic materials. The optimal selection of organic material and dehydrating material for preparing nanosized graphene is essential for obtaining the best results. Due to these surveys, D-fructose as organic material was the perfect choice for attaining graphene (GQDs). Fructose with a structure like all simple sugar can be expressed as a six-carbon linear chain with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups with the formula (C6H12O6) [9]. Hydrogen functionalization is a high possibility to be achieved if hydrochloric acid is used, which has a significant role in the dehydration process [10].

Controlling optical and electrical properties produces high efficiency in optoelectronic applications through the close correlation between size and energy bandgap [11]. The literature review of studies preparing multicolor GQDs with uniform size distribution and recording the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) reveals that the use of the abovementioned methods is rare. For example, Taghi et al. reported on the synthesis of NGQDs using a hydrothermal method and obtained an energy bandgap of approximately 1.91 eV [12]. Bayat et al. created SLGQDs using a hydrothermal method and acquired bandgaps of approximately 2.9 eV and 3.1 eV from cyclic voltammetry and optical measurements, correspondingly [13].

In this article, we synthesized and characterized GQDs using D-fructose as precursor material. The effect of hydrochloric acid as a dehydrated factor on the reaction mechanism was monitored, and we briefly studied the dependence of the energy gap on the size of the GQDs. The effect of the functionalization groups during the reaction of prepared GQDs was also investigated. The HOMO and LOMO of multicolor GQDs were procured using CV voltammetry.

Hydrogen chloride solution, hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%), and D-fructose (C6H12O6, ≥99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. No additional purification has been used for all bought chemicals, and deionized water was used for the preparation of all solutions.

The GQDs were prepared using a typical dehydration method. First, the stock solution was prepared: 10 g D-fructose was dissolved in 200 ml of DI-grade water. The solution was heated to 85 °C while stirring for 30 min. Then, 12 ml of HCl was added to the solution. After 30 min of observing the color change of the solution, a light-yellow solution was gained, and a dark-yellow solution was reached after another 20 min. At each color change during the reaction, the solution was transferred to a capped glass bottle and kept at room temperature ready for measurement.

Using a high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) accessory and field emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30), the microstructure and morphology of the GQDs samples were examined. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were determined using a GENESYS 10S UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The emission of light colors was observed in the dark box, and the emission curve was realized using an LS 55 Fluorescence Spectrometer. The composition and structure of the GQDs were characterized using nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy (Ascend 850). Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) investigation was carried off on an FT-IR Spectrum 100 (PerkinElmer, USA). Raman spectra were used (BRUKER, Germany, SENTERRA) to demonstrate how the graphene structure formed. Three electrodes were employed for the CV measurements: a wire of platinum was utilized as the counter electrode, the working electrode was made of glassy carbon and GQDs, and Ag/AgCl was exploited as a reference electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design, Synthesis, and Mechanism of the GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized GQDs samples were prepared using the suggested facile strategy shown in Fig. 1 . The equipment of GQDs with a hydrogen-rich surface was carried out via a dehydration method using D-fructose as the starting material. GQDs were captured using the HCl-assisted dehydration method at 85 °C for 2 h. When hydrogen atoms are redistributed in a solution with oxygen atoms present in fructose molecules, they tend to form shells with other hydrogen atoms in order to achieve chemical stability and the formation of a water molecule. This process is facilitated by the presence of HCl. HCl does not tend to react with hydroxyl groups in D-fructose as a substitution reaction but it is able to make mild dehydration for D-fructose (HCl, as a hygroscopic solution) which keeps the carbon atoms isolated after stripping them of all the elements to produce GQDs, as presented in Fig. 1.

The color gradation of the reaction mixture (from yellow to yellowish purple) is shown in Fig. 2 indicating an increasing GQD size. In the current study, the increasing sizes of synthesized GQDs have appeared during the reaction development, where the temperature was kept constant. Samples that achieved a significant color change will be the focus of this article, and optimal samples will be established.

XRD analysis was applied to investigate the GQD crystalline structures. The GQDs structure ranges from crystalline to amorphous structure according to method preparation. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the precursor material D-fructose powder diffraction peaks appear at angle 2θ=
24.80
o
[16]; the focus has been placed on this angle because the monitoring of change starts from this range, which agrees with the reference study of XRD GQDs [13–15]. The change in structure that occurred after the addition of HCl was observed.

During the reaction and the growth of the GQDs, there were some changes in the material structure formation. Comparing the diffraction pattern of raw D-fructose powder with the diffraction pattern of the growing GQDs is required to find out the final structure of the GQDs. Fig. 3(b) represents the diffraction peak for the GQDs with size 1.9 nm recorded at 2θ = 27.5°, which was near that of the raw D-fructose powder at 2θ = 22°. This demonstrates how dehydration causes the network of carbon atoms to be damaged and then restored. During the reaction, the size became 3 nm, and an orange color was observed, which reveals that the size is larger and that the process of graphene formation has become more explored. The 002 peaks of the GQDs moved to a lower angle of 2θ = 23.5° with size 5 nm and were broader than in the case of the reaction initiation, proposing an increase in interlayer space after functionalization with HCl [15].

UV–Vis spectra of different GQDs peaks were highlighted in the range 200–400 nm. The GQDs UV–Vis spectrum analysis pinpoints two distinguished types of peaks as follows: a strong peak at wavelength 232 nm is referred to as П → П*, which is seen because of the aromatic C = C structure, and the shoulder peaks at wavelengths 304 nm, 309 nm, and 325 nm are related to the electron transition from n → П* simply because of the oxygen-containing groups on GQDs surfaces forming C = O [17,18]. The absorption peaks are depicted in Fig. 4 . The shift in the absorbance peaks during the reaction explains that this may be due to the reaction time effect during growth, which appeared on the absorption spectrum of GQDs. The dark blue peak represents the first extracted batch of the solution and the smallest size of GQDs formed. The green peak symbolizes the second extracted batch and the second size = 3 nm of GQDs formed, and the yellow peak stands for the third extracted batch and the third biggest size = 5nm of GQDs formed.

It was recognized that, with the change in the sizes of the GQDs, the intensity of the highest peak decreased with the stability of the position. The shift of the lower peaks represented the formation of GQDs. The tail in the absorption curve reflects defects, impurities, and other functional groups present in the structure that influenced the electronic transitions, which is the purpose of converting graphene with a zero bandgap to GQDs. This may be further discussed in a later section.

PL spectrum measurement is very powerful for examining the emission spectrum of GQDs. For a representative measurement, a GQD sample is excited with an appropriate excitation wavelength, so the GQDs emit corresponding emission peaks. Fig. 5 (b) displays photos of the GQDs samples taken inside the dark box. The colors that GQDs mirror, when exposed to ultraviolet light, cannot be seen in visible light. The synthesized GQDs PL spectra samples are illustrated in Fig. 5(a). The GQD samples, when excited by 360 nm wavelength energy, resulted in a PL, with emission peaks at 400, 410, and 460 nm, identifying the most graphene quantum dots [4,19,20].

It is possible that the redshift of emission peaks is connected to the GQDs' shifting sizes as a result of wavelength. The electronic transition from the excited level to the initial ground level caused all of the marked peaks [19].

As depicted in Fig. 6 , for the smallest size (1.9 nm) GQDs, the exciting wavelength changed from 350 to 430 nm. The PL intensity significantly increased; however, the fluorescent peak at 512 nm did not exhibit any shift. This exciting-independent PL expressed a comparable pattern to the majority of the reported GQDs [21].

FT-IR analysis was applied to the detection of the chemical bonds of the samples, as illustrated in Fig. 7 . The FT-IR spectra of GQD absorption are then performed in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 since here most organic compounds absorb radiation. Fig. 7 exposes the broad absorption peak between 3100 and 3484 cm−1 for three samples taken at different sizes, confirming the increase in the degree of the hydroxy group–bonded OH stretch O–H bonds. Because of the development of carboxyl C = O on the surface/ edge, the peak at 1711 cm−1 was more prevalent. GQDs were formed as peaks around 1036 cm−1 (O-C-O) steadily decreased, and the observation of bands around 2177 cm−1 led to the formation of honeycomb GQDs [22]. It is noticeable that absorption starts at a lower depth. Then, as the GQDs size rises, the depth of absorption becomes more apparent. The peak that denotes C–H at 2922 cm−1 demonstrates that this bond is formed clearly, implying the hydrogen-functionalized GQDs [23]. Also, the formation of these functional groups supports the idea of a hybrid structure for the associated graphene core and edge groups [4]. The formation of these functional groups at the edges reduces the energy gap with increasing size and vice versa [3,11,24]. The current results agree with previous publications.

An accurate and nondestructive method for characterizing carbon-based nanomaterials is Raman spectroscopy [25]. It provides information on the crystal lattice structure of the materials. Fig. 8 manifests the Raman spectrum of produced GQDs in various sizes, as recorded in the spectral region between 1200 and 2800 cm−1. Two distinctive Raman peaks can be seen in the spectra for the three samples positioned at approximately 1350 cm−1 and 1595 cm−1, which, respectively, correspond to the GQDs D and G bands [25,26]. It is expected that when D-fructose is dehydrated, the majority of the functional groups linked to the carbon atom are removed, and the majority of the damaged sp2 carbon conjugation is restored. The greater intensity of the G band in this study compared to the D band suggests that the broken sp2-carbon conjugated network is repaired, inducing high graphitization of the GQDs that were produced using HCl [27,28]. The increased G band further supports the greatest degree of D-fructose dehydration achieved by deoxygenation with an HCl solution [29].

The prepared high-quality GQDs and the value of the current synthetic technique for effective GQD preparation are both delineated in the present case by the relative intensity of the “disorder” D band to the crystalline G band for each size (ID/IG = 0.5) [30–32]. The Raman band of these samples also displays a (D + G) band, pointing out multilayered graphene sheets; this band shifts to higher wavenumbers at 3000 cm−1 [30].

To achieve the nature in terms of GQD size versus the energy gap [11–13], a TEM analysis has been executed to accurately estimate the size distribution for each sample. Fig. 9 presents the images, size distributions, and distinct crystal lattices of the GQDs for sizes 1.9 nm, 3 nm, and 5 nm, respectively.

The rising size influence of GQDs during the reaction is depicted in Fig. 9. Following the dehydration of hydrogen atoms attached to the carbon atoms that make up the precursor material (D-fructose), the reaction initially had an average distribution of tiny size equivalent to 1.9 nm to create the first GQDs. Following GQDs with an average size distribution of 5 nm and an equivalent lattice plane distance parameter of 0.24 nm, it was noted that the plane distance parameter was equal to 0.18 nm for the smallest GQD size, signifying considerable crystallinity [33,43].

The absorbance (α) obtained from UV–Vis spectroscopy in Fig. 4 has been multiplied by (hv)2 and plotted against energy hv using Tau'c method plots [4,13–34]. The calculated values of E_g were realized by extrapolating the tangent to intersect the x-axis in Fig. 10 (a), while the relationship between the size of GQDs and their corresponding E_g values is displayed in Fig. 10(b).

It is apparent from Fig. 10(b) that the increase in particle size decreases with the energy bandgap; this may be due to the chemical functionalization of GQDs which agrees well with that discussed in previous studies [35,36]. Furthermore, the
E g
obtained of GQDs using photoluminescence analysis can be determined based on
E g
= 1240/ λ [13] and compared to the energy gap related to absorbance measurement spectra according to Table.1 . The disparity in Eg values shows that the Eg attained through luminescence measurement originated from localized state emission down to the lower ground level, whereas the Eg acquired by absorption is a direct outcome of exciton absorption or a change from HOMO to LUMO.

NMR spectroscopy was executed to accurately identify the elements and functional groups that were detected in the GQDs and to compare these results with those described previously in this study. Fig. 11 embodies the 1H- NMR spectrum for the smallest size (1.9 nm) GQDs; it describes the presence of a number of absorptions of different protons at
δ H
1.99, 2.13, 2.51, and 2.87 ppm, which revealed the existence of paraffinic hydrogen. In addition, the presence of signals at 6.58, 7.43, 8.13, and 9.36 ppm reflected the presence of olefinic protons. The above deduction is supported by the FT-IR absorption spectrum at 1671 cm−1 and 2923 cm−1. The 13C NMR spectrum of GQDs is portrayed in Fig. 12 . 13C NMR signals of GQDs recorded at
δ C
180.5, 165.3, 161.3, and 151.8 ppm exemplified the presence of olefinic carbons. In the middle of the 1H NMR spectrum, several absorptions in the range of 3.39 – 4.22 ppm supported the presence of oxygenated methane and methylene D-fructose. The 13C NMR absorption at 60–100 ppm supported this deduction. These results are in line with previous studies [15,37–39].

CV is a useful electrochemical technique for determining the HOMO and LOMO energy levels of GQDs. Cathodic and anodic currents, respectively, suddenly start to emerge when a potential is created above the LOMO and below the HOMO [40,41]. Any CV curve displayed the cathodic and anodic peaks, which were the primary properties of the electrode material [14]. Multicolor GQDs suspension was drop-cast into an electrode to prepare it for this study (4 μl) and nafion (0.4 μl, 4% w) onto a glassy carbon electrode and dried using an infrared ray. The oxidation and reduction peaks related to electron and hole injections into the conduction and valence bands were accordingly utilized to find out the
E
HOMO
and
E
LOMO
using the following equations [42]:
E
HOMO
= - e
(
E
Ox . v s . N H E
+ 4.75 )
( e V )
E
LOMO
= - e
(
E
Red . v s . N H E
+ 4.75 )
( e V )
E
NHE
= - e
(
E
Ag / A g c l
+ 0.197 )

The GQDs/glassy carbon electrode exhibited a reduction peak and an oxidation peak at countless sizes corresponding to values of
E
HOMO
and
E
LOMO
and finally calculating the band-gap energy can be presented in Fig. 13 (a).
Fig. 13 illustrates the glassy carbon electrode behavior in the presence/absence of the three sizes of GQDs.

In this work, blue-green-yellow-photoluminescent GQDs were successfully synthesized by the dehydration method at a particular fixed temperature using only D-fructose powder as precursors and hydrochloric acid in the presence of DI. The GQDs characterization analysis of the proposed method gives similar results to previous investigations for GQDs synthesized using different methods. Using a cyclic voltammetry method, the LUMO and HOMO energy levels of multicolor GQDs were determined versus a vacuum. The multicolor GQD glassy carbon electrode provides accurate results and is generally consistent with optical tests, which demonstrate that the energy bandgap narrows as GQD size grows. The GQDs bandgap decreases from 3.4 to 1.51 eV when a GQD is made functional by GQD-OOH. Furthermore, the process development of reaction and the resulting functionalization has been explored and discussed thoroughly employing FTIR and NMR techniques. Defects and structural change were examined using the Raman technique. Origins of emission emerging from the defects were measured and discussed. This product due to its unique properties can be used in many applications such as organic light-emitting diodes and organic photovoltaic devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
